01_initial_request.target_name: Distributed Secondary Control for Current Sharing and Voltage Restoration in DC Microgrid
01_initial_request.input_data: DC microgrid with N parallel DC/DC converters connected to a DC bus; droop control parameters (gains k_i, line resistances R_i); nominal DC bus voltage V* = 48V (simulation) / 100V (experiment); converter ratings and communication graph topology.
01_initial_request.user_intent: To solve the inherent trade-off in DC microgrid droop control between current sharing accuracy and DC bus voltage deviation. The high droop gain needed for good current sharing causes unacceptable voltage deviation from the nominal value, requiring a secondary control layer that can simultaneously achieve both objectives without requiring DC bus voltage feedback.
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: 1) Restore DC bus voltage to nominal value (48V in simulation, 100V in experiment); 2) Achieve proportional current sharing among converters with ratio I_i/I_j = k_j/k_i; 3) Handle both resistance loads and constant power loads (CPLs); 4) Support plug-and-play operation; 5) Maintain performance with communication time delays up to 50ms.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Droop control is widely used for current sharing in DC microgrids by setting a 'virtual resistance' (droop gain) much larger than line resistance, ensuring I_i/I_j proportional to k_j/k_i. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: High droop gain causes large DC bus voltage deviation (V_B = V* - (R_i + k_i)I_i), creating a fundamental trade-off. Existing centralized secondary control suffers from single-point failures, while existing distributed methods require DC bus voltage feedback or cannot handle CPLs. [Decision] To address this, chose to analyze the droop control model mathematically and define a new parameter 'virtual voltage drop' λ_i = (R_i + k_i)I_i, whose average value λ_avg plays a key role in achieving both current sharing and voltage restoration simultaneously.

parameters.model: DC microgrid with N converters, droop control V_ref_i = V* - k_i * I_i
parameters.current_sharing_ratio: I_i/I_j = (R_j + k_j)/(R_i + k_i) ≈ k_j/k_i when k_i >> R_i
parameters.virtual_voltage_drop: λ_i = (R_i + k_i)I_i, and V_B = V* - λ_i for all i

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The DC bus voltage deviation V* - V_B equals the virtual voltage drop λ_i for every converter. Therefore, if all converters agree on the same λ_i (i.e., λ_i = λ_avg), both current sharing (from proportional λ_i) and voltage restoration (from V_B = V* - λ_avg → V* when λ_avg → 0) can be achieved. The average value λ_avg = (1/N)Σλ_i = (1/N)Σ(R_i + k_i)I_i is the key quantity to estimate.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The virtual voltage drop λ_i has been defined, and the average value λ_avg is identified as the key to achieving both objectives. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Computing λ_avg requires global information (all λ_i values), which is not available to individual converters in a distributed setting. [Decision] To enable distributed estimation of λ_avg, chose dynamic average consensus algorithms. Designed a secondary controller where each converter maintains an internal state x_i and exchanges only the control input u_i with neighbors, using the consensus update: ẋ_i = -αx_i + βΣ_{j∈N_i}(u_i - u_j), u_i = x_i + λ_i.

parameters.controller_equations: ẋ_i = -αx_i + β·Σ_{j∈N_i} a_{ij}(u_i - u_j), u_i = x_i + λ_i
parameters.communication_requirement: Strongly connected and weight-balanced graph; each converter exchanges only u_i with neighbors
parameters.feedback_requirement: No DC bus voltage feedback needed; only local current I_i measurement

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The proposed controller estimates λ_avg without requiring global information or DC bus voltage measurement. Theorem 2 proves that with this controller, when the communication graph is strongly connected and weight-balanced, the control objectives (current sharing and voltage restoration) are achieved with bounded tracking error that can be made arbitrarily small by increasing β.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The distributed secondary controller has been designed with theoretical guarantees (Theorem 2). [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The theoretical results need to be validated through numerical simulation under various load conditions and operational scenarios. [Decision] To validate the proposed scheme, designed five case studies in MATLAB Simulink: (1) Resistance load, (2) Constant power loads, (3) Plug-in and plug-out, (4) Comparison with existing methods, (5) Multiple buses.

parameters.system: 4 DC/DC bulk converters connected to DC bus, V* = 48V
parameters.test_scenarios: 5 case studies with varying loads, converter plug-in/out, and comparison with [22] and [24]

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Case 1 (Resistance load): Bus voltage restored from 42V to 48V after secondary control activated; proper current sharing maintained. Case 2 (CPL): Bus voltage well maintained despite varying CPL. Case 3 (Plug-and-play): Current properly redistributed when converter disconnected/reconnected. Case 4 (Comparison): Our scheme outperforms [22] and [24] when no converter has bus voltage access. Case 5 (Multiple buses): Both bus voltages maintained near 48V.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: All five simulation case studies validated the effectiveness of the proposed scheme. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Simulation results need to be confirmed through physical experiments to verify practical feasibility. [Decision] To experimentally validate the scheme, built a DC microgrid testbed with 3 DC/DC boost converters controlled by dSPACE1006, with sampling and PWM frequency at 20kHz, testing resistance loads, CPLs, plug-and-play, and time delay effects.

tool.name: dSPACE1006 control platform + DC/DC boost converters

parameters.test_cases: Resistance load change, CPL, plug-and-play, time delay (1ms, 10ms, 50ms)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Resistance load: Bus voltage restored to 100V from 96V despite load changes (25Ω→50Ω→25Ω). CPL: Voltage maintained with proper current sharing during CPL connection/disconnection. Plug-and-play: Seamless converter addition without voltage/current disruption. Time delay: Satisfactory performance with delays up to 50ms, confirming practical communication feasibility.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Both simulation and experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed distributed secondary control scheme. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Need to analyze the robustness and limitations, particularly regarding parameter sensitivity and communication requirements. [Decision] To complete the analysis, evaluated the trade-offs in parameter selection (α, β) and compared communication requirements with existing methods. Larger α and β improve performance but increase sensitivity to measurement errors and time delays.

parameters.trade_off_analysis: Increasing α, β → better performance but more sensitive to noise and delays
parameters.communication_comparison: Our scheme requires only u_i exchange (1 signal) vs. existing methods requiring both I_i and V_B
parameters.load_capability: Handles both resistance loads and CPLs, unlike existing methods limited to resistance loads

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The proposed scheme has three key advantages: 1) No DC bus voltage feedback required, simplifying implementation; 2) Handles both resistance loads and CPLs; 3) Simpler parameter tuning compared to existing methods with complex stability conditions. Future work includes extending to more complex topologies and analyzing time delay effects theoretically.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Comprehensive validation through 5 simulation case studies in MATLAB Simulink and 4 experimental test cases on a dSPACE1006-controlled hardware platform with 3 DC/DC boost converters.

Voltage Restoration Accuracy.interpretation: DC bus voltage restored to nominal value (48V simulation, 100V experiment) with zero steady-state error after secondary control activation.

Current Sharing Accuracy.interpretation: Proportional current sharing maintained according to design ratio I_i/I_j = k_j/k_i across all load conditions.

Plug-and-Play Recovery Time.interpretation: System quickly re-stabilizes after converter connection/disconnection events.

Communication Delay Tolerance.interpretation: Satisfactory control performance maintained with communication delays up to 50ms, sufficient for cellular network implementation.

Comparison Improvement.interpretation: When no converter has bus voltage access, the proposed scheme eliminates the stable voltage violation error present in existing methods.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The proposed distributed secondary control scheme successfully achieves both current sharing and voltage restoration in DC microgrids without requiring DC bus voltage feedback. The scheme handles both resistance loads and CPLs, supports plug-and-play operation, and maintains performance with practical communication delays. Both simulation and experimental results validate the theoretical guarantees.
